title: Top 10 Original Netflix Movies | The Big Picture
format: Sean and Amanda build a 10-film Netflix Original Hall of Fame, then Sean talks to Alex Garland about 'Devs.'

segments.heading: What is a Netflix original, anyway?

films.title: Molly's Game
films.note: Not a Netflix production, but found a second life on the service, which the hosts say explains a lot about what draws filmmakers there in the first place.

films.title: Spenser Confidential
films.note: Watched by more people than 'Tiger King' per Netflix's own earnings call, despite pacing and tone problems the hosts say plague a lot of these movies.

films.title: The Irishman
films.note: Cited as part of the awards-season case for Netflix, alongside 'Marriage Story' and 'American Factory.'

films.title: Beasts of No Nation
films.note: Netflix's first feature, five years old at the time of this taping. Amanda: 'I remember thinking, wow, this feels weirdly unfinished.'

films.title: Set It Up
films.note: Amanda's pick for what Netflix could have led with instead of 'Beasts of No Nation': a movie about a workplace romance at a sports-media company that hits close to home for two Ringer employees.

films.title: The Cloverfield Paradox
films.note: The surprise Super Bowl drop the hosts cite as the bad version of Netflix swooping in to rescue a stalled studio project.

films.title: The Lovebirds
films.note: Paramount's pandemic-era release-date casualty, picked up by Netflix in the same kind of rescue move that landed 'Triple Frontier.'

films.title: Triple Frontier
films.note: Financed by Netflix rather than rescued, which Sean insists still counts.

films.title: Roma
films.note: Named as proof the prestige and awards push is still very much alive alongside the teen-rom-com side of the business.

films.title: Circus of Books
films.note: Documentary about the L.A. LGBTQ bookstore, released the same week as 'The Willoughbys.'

films.title: The Willoughbys
films.note: Amanda keeps mistaking the trailer for a British period drama; it's an animated kids' movie.

segments.heading: Extraction
segments.note: Chris Hemsworth, one very long shootout

films.title: Extraction
films.note: Directed by stuntman-turned-director Sam Hargrave, produced by the Russo brothers. Amanda's husband ended up watching the whole thing while she cooked dinner and narrating the kills to her; both call it a convincing, disposable action movie built around a 12-minute one-take-style shootout. Compared unfavorably in rewatch value to '6 Underground.'

segments.heading: The Netflix Films Hall of Fame
segments.note: 10 spots, one rule: one movie per director

films.title: Roma
films.note: In immediately, no debate, along with 'The Irishman' and 'Marriage Story': the three the hosts agree deserve their own wing but get folded in anyway.

films.title: The Irishman
films.note: One of the three locks.

films.title: Marriage Story
films.note: The third lock. Disqualifies 'The Meyerowitz Stories' under the one-film-per-director rule, over Sean's objections.

films.title: Shirkers
films.note: Sandi Tan's documentary about a lost film she made as a teenager and rediscovered decades later. Amanda's find. She made her husband watch it the same night.

films.title: American Factory
films.note: Called an uncomplicated choice.

films.title: To All the Boys I've Loved Before
films.note: Wins the one-slot showdown with 'Set It Up.' Amanda argues it did more to build a teen audience and a new genre for the service, even though she personally prefers 'Set It Up.' Their producer's vote breaks the tie.

films.title: Okja
films.note: Traded in for 'The Ballad of Buster Scruggs' in a mid-show negotiation. Amanda loves it; Sean says he 'didn't feel complete' watching it, despite Tilda Swinton, Jake Gyllenhaal, and Giancarlo Esposito.

films.title: Triple Frontier
films.note: Sean will not let this one go: 'are we the only people left on Triple Frontier Island?'

films.title: The Ballad of Buster Scruggs
films.note: The Coen brothers movie Sean insisted on, traded for Okja's spot.

films.title: Icarus
films.note: Covers the Oscar-bait/documentary case generally: the hosts use it to justify cutting '13th,' 'Crip Camp: A Disability Revolution,' and 'What Happened, Miss Simone?' from the final 10.

segments.heading: The ones that didn't make it

films.title: Happy as Lazzaro
films.note: Alice Rohrwacher's film. Both agree it's very good but a tough home watch. Amanda calls it more of an 'event' movie.

films.title: Atlantics
films.note: Mati Diop's film. Sean argues it deserves more of a case than 'Happy as Lazzaro' but it gets bumped anyway.

films.title: Crip Camp: A Disability Revolution
films.note: Produced by the Obamas' Higher Ground; both find it well made and educational but not quite at 'American Factory' level.

films.title: The Two Popes
films.note: Sean recalls a Telluride crowd predicting a Best Picture win; it didn't happen.

films.title: Fyre
films.note: Chris Smith's documentary, discussed alongside the competing Hulu Fyre Festival doc. Sean: 'this is kind of like watching eight really good YouTube videos in a row.'

films.title: Velvet Buzzsaw
films.note: Dan Gilroy's art-world horror. Sean: 'imperfect,' with 'a very strange Jake Gyllenhaal performance.'

films.title: 13th
films.note: Ava DuVernay's documentary. Called hugely important to Netflix's history ('there is no Crip Camp: A Disability Revolution without 13th'), but it loses its spot to Icarus.

films.title: Mudbound
films.note: Dee Rees's film, which Amanda never actually finished.

films.title: Private Life
films.note: Tamara Jenkins's film, starring Kathryn Hahn and Paul Giamatti. Both call it underseen and well-reviewed, but not representative enough of the broader Netflix slate to make the cut.

films.title: The Perfection
films.note: Starring Allison Williams. Sean: 'disruptive and fucked up and also fascinating.'

films.title: The Highwaymen
films.note: Kevin Costner and Woody Harrelson hunting Bonnie and Clyde, directed by John Lee Hancock.

films.title: Wine Country
films.note: Amy Poehler's comedy.

films.title: War Machine
films.note: Brad Pitt's first movie for a streaming service, called an important precedent for the industry even though the film itself landed poorly.

films.title: The Meyerowitz Stories (New and Selected)
films.note: Sean's preferred Baumbach film, disqualified once 'Marriage Story' takes the one-per-director slot.

films.title: I Lost My Body
films.note: The animated film, called good but not worthy of a spot.

films.title: High Flying Bird
films.note: Soderbergh's iPhone-shot film. Called the more 'important' of his two 2019 Netflix movies, and briefly pinned before losing its place.

films.title: The Laundromat
films.note: Soderbergh's other 2019 release.

films.title: Gerald's Game
films.note: Mike Flanagan's Stephen King adaptation with Carla Gugino. Called phenomenal but not top-10 material.

films.title: Win It All
films.note: Joe Swanberg's film with Jake Johnson.

films.title: They'll Love Me When I'm Dead
films.note: Documentary about the making of Orson Welles's 'The Other Side of the Wind.'

films.title: Dolemite Is My Name
films.note: Eddie Murphy's comeback vehicle.

films.title: What Happened, Miss Simone?
films.note: Nominated for the Best Documentary Oscar the same year as 'Amy,' called possibly the first Netflix Oscar nomination.

films.title: 6 Underground
films.note: Michael Bay's film, used as the yardstick for how much less coherent 'Extraction' feels by comparison.

films.title: Tigertail
films.note: Alan Yang's directorial debut. Both admire the craft but feel its influences (Edward Yang, Wong Kar-wai) show through more than the movie's own voice.

films.title: Miss Americana
films.note: The Taylor Swift documentary. Amanda brings it up as a late add to acknowledge Netflix's music-doc lane; Sean rules it out immediately.

films: Winter on Fire: Ukraine's Fight for Freedom

To All the Boys I've Loved Before.title: To All the Boys I've Loved Before

They'll Love Me When I'm Dead.title: They'll Love Me When I'm Dead

Winter on Fire: Ukraine's Fight for Free.title: Winter on Fire: Ukraine's Fight for Freedom
